g.data-narration: Welcome to Heaps and Heapsort. This algorithm guarantees O(n log n) sorting with O(1) space complexity.
g.text: Efficient O(n log n) Comparison-Based Sorting Algorithm

g.data-narration: A heap is a complete binary tree satisfying the heap property: parent ≥ children in max-heap, parent ≤ children in min-heap.
g.text: All levels filled except possibly last

g.data-narration: In max-heap, the root contains the maximum element. In min-heap, the root contains the minimum element.
A complete binary tree has all levels filled except possibly the last, with nodes filled left to right. This ensures minimum height.
g.text: Not Complete: ✗ Gaps in last level

g.data-narration: For a node at index i (0-based): parent is ⌊(i-1)/2⌋, left child is 2i+1, and right child is 2i+2.
Heapify compares a node with its children, swapping with the larger child if heap property is violated, then continues downward.
g.text: largest = i; compare with children; swap if needed; recurse
Time Complexity: O(log n) - height of tree

g.data-narration: Build Heap applies heapify starting from the last non-leaf node (index ⌊n/2⌋-1) down to the root, taking O(n) time.
g.text: BUILD-HEAP: for i = ⌊n/2⌋-1 down to 0: HEAPIFY(A, n, i)
Why O(n) not O(n log n)?
More work at bottom (many nodes), less work at top (fewer nodes). Mathematical sum converges to O(n).

g.data-narration: Heap Insert adds element at end then sifts up. Extract Max removes root, replaces with last element, then heapifies.
g.text: Add at end → Compare with parent → Swap if needed → Repeat
Remove root → Replace with last → Heapify from root → Return removed

g.data-narration: Heapsort builds a max-heap, then repeatedly swaps root with last element and heapifies the reduced heap.
g.text: BUILD-HEAP(A, n) → for i = n-1 to 1: swap A[0],A[i] → HEAPIFY(A, i, 0)

g.data-narration: Example: Build max heap from [4, 10, 3, 5, 1]. After heapify operations, final heap is [10, 5, 3, 4, 1].
Heapsort on [12, 11, 13, 5, 6, 7] produces [5, 6, 7, 11, 12, 13] through repeated extraction and heapify.
Heaps implement priority queues: Insert(15), Insert(10), Insert(20), ExtractMax(), Insert(25), ExtractMax() results in [15, 10].
g.text: Operations: Insert(15) → Insert(10) → Insert(20) → ExtractMax() → Insert(25) → ExtractMax()

g.data-narration: Heapsort has O(n log n) time and O(1) space complexity. Heap operations take O(log n) time.
Heapsort guarantees O(n log n) worst-case but is not stable. Quicksort is faster in practice but has O(n²) worst-case.
Key takeaways: Heapsort guarantees O(n log n), uses O(1) space, and is not stable. Heaps power priority queues in Dijkstra's and Huffman coding.
g.text: Heapsort: O(n log n) worst-case, O(1) space, not stable | Priority Queue: O(log n) insert/extract, O(1) peek
